title: Why Smart People Feel Like Frauds: The Psychology of Impostor Syndrome and Its Hidden Benefits
description:
in Life, Psychology |   	March 23rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Incom┬Łpe┬Łtent peo┬Łple tend to see them┬Łselves as not just com┬Łpe┬Łtent, but high
body:
in Life, Psychology |   	March 23rd, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Incom┬Łpe┬Łtent peo┬Łple tend to see them┬Łselves as not just com┬Łpe┬Łtent, but high┬Łly com┬Łpe┬Łtent. So, at any rate, holds the the┬Ło┬Łry of the ŌĆ£Dun┬Łning-Kruger effect,ŌĆØ pre┬Łvi┬Łous┬Łly fea┬Łtured here on Open Cul┬Łture. But does the con┬Łverse also hold: do high┬Łly com┬Łpe┬Łtent peo┬Łple tend to see them┬Łselves as incom┬Łpe┬Łtent? That would seem to be an impli┬Łca┬Łtion of whatŌĆÖs been called ŌĆ£impos┬Łtor syn┬Łdrome,ŌĆØ a per┬Łsis┬Łtent sense of inad┬Łe┬Łqua┬Łcy rel┬Ła┬Łtive to oneŌĆÖs sta┬Łtus or posi┬Łtion, unsup┬Łport┬Łed by any objec┬Łtive evi┬Łdence. If you your┬Łself have been afflict┬Łed with that con┬Łdi┬Łtion, it may be a tad hasty to take it as a sign of your own effec┬Łtive┬Łness, but as the Har┬Łvard Busi┬Łness SchoolŌĆÖs Arthur C. Brooks explains in the clip above, it may nonethe┬Łless ben┬Łe┬Łfit you to lean into it.
ŌĆ£What all strivers IŌĆÖve ever met have in com┬Łmon is that, the high┬Łer they climb and the more suc┬Łcess they have, the more inse┬Łcure they feel in their own suc┬Łcess because theyŌĆÖre not quite sure that theyŌĆÖve earned it or deserve it,ŌĆØ Brooks says. Iron┬Łi┬Łcal┬Łly, in his expe┬Łri┬Łence, ŌĆ£peo┬Łple who deserve suc┬Łcess through hard work and mer┬Łit and per┬Łson┬Łal respon┬Łsi┬Łbil┬Łi┬Łty are not quite sure they deserve it, and the peo┬Łple who donŌĆÖt deserve it are often the peo┬Łple who are actu┬Łal┬Łly most sure that they do.ŌĆØ
In that last group are pos┬Łses┬Łsors of the so-called ŌĆ£dark tri┬ŁadŌĆØ traits: nar┬Łcis┬Łsism, Machi┬Łavel┬Łlian┬Łism, and psy┬Łchopa┬Łthy. A ŌĆ£good, nor┬Łmal, healthy per┬Łson,ŌĆØ by con┬Łtrast, will nat┬Łu┬Łral┬Łly won┬Łder if they real┬Łly mer┬Łit the pro┬Łmo┬Łtions, rewards, and acco┬Łlades they receive, and if theyŌĆÖre tru┬Łly up to each task ahead.
To com┬Łbat impos┬Łtor syn┬Łdrome, Brooks rec┬Łom┬Łmends you┬ĀŌĆ£under┬Łstand it, keep up to date with it, and keep try┬Łing to get bet┬Łter at the things youŌĆÖre not good at yet.ŌĆØ Human┬Łi┬ŁtyŌĆÖs gen┬Łer┬Łal neg┬Ła┬Łtiv┬Łi┬Łty bias may keep most of us sus┬Łpect┬Łing that weŌĆÖve been over┬Łes┬Łti┬Łmat┬Łed, but that does┬ŁnŌĆÖt mean we should ignore the oppor┬Łtu┬Łni┬Łties for gen┬Łuine self-improve┬Łment that such feel┬Łings present. ŌĆ£The truth is, if things are real┬Łly, real┬Łly rough for you, youŌĆÖre not all weak┬Łness┬Łes, and if things are going real┬Łly well for you, youŌĆÖre not all strengths.ŌĆØ It just hap┬Łpens that at some times, every┬Łone focus┬Łes on the for┬Łmer, and at oth┬Łer times, the lat┬Łter, and whatŌĆÖs impor┬Łtant is not to let your┬Łself be too heav┬Łi┬Łly influ┬Łenced in either case. Per┬Łhaps you can stay ground┬Łed by bear┬Łing in mind a cou┬Łple of┬Ātrusty old adages: that nobodyŌĆÖs per┬Łfect, and that you do, some┬Łtimes, have to fake it ŌĆÖtil you make it.
Relat┬Łed con┬Łtent:
24 Com┬Łmon Cog┬Łni┬Łtive Bias┬Łes: A Visu┬Łal List of the Psy┬Łcho┬Łlog┬Łi┬Łcal Sys┬Łtems Errors That Keep Us From Think┬Łing Ratio┬Łnal┬Łly
Why Incom┬Łpe┬Łtent Peo┬Łple Think TheyŌĆÖre Com┬Łpe┬Łtent: The Dun┬Łning-Kruger Effect, Explained
Why Over┬Łcon┬Łfi┬Łdence Is Our Most Dan┬Łger┬Łous Cog┬Łni┬Łtive Bias
John Cleese on How ŌĆ£Stu┬Łpid Peo┬Łple Have No Idea How Stu┬Łpid They AreŌĆØ (Oth┬Łer┬Łwise Known as the Dun┬Łning-Kruger Effect)
There Are Eight Forms of Intel┬Łli┬Łgence, Not Just One: Which Apply to You?
The Sur┬Łpris┬Łing Pow┬Łer of Bore┬Łdom: It Lets You Con┬Łfront Big Ques┬Łtions & Give Life Mean┬Łing
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: Watch Jazzy Spies: 1969 Psychedelic Sesame Street Animation, Featuring Grace Slick, Teaches Kids to Count
description:
in Animation, Music, Television |   	March 24th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When asked for their favorite Sesame Street seg┬Łment, many chil┬Łdre
body:
in Animation, Music, Television |   	March 24th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When asked for their favorite Sesame Street seg┬Łment, many chil┬Łdren of the 70s and 80s point to┬ĀPin┬Łball Num┬Łber Count. Psy┬Łche┬Łdel┬Łic ani┬Łma┬Łtion, the Point┬Łer Sis┬Łters, odd time signaturesŌĆöwhatŌĆÖs not to love? But for the seri┬Łous Sesame Street buff, the ŌĆ£Jazz Num┬ŁbersŌĆØ series above deserves the sil┬Łver medal. ItŌĆÖs got free jazz, Yel┬Łlow Sub┬Łma┬Łrine-style sur┬Łre┬Łal┬Łis┬Łtic ani┬Łma┬Łtion, and a vocal from Grace Slick of Jef┬Łfer┬Łson Air┬Łplane. How many young par┬Łents rec┬Łog┬Łnized her dis┬Łtinc┬Łtive voice, I won┬Łder?
Also known as ŌĆ£Jazzy Spies,ŌĆØ this 1969 series of ani┬Łma┬Łtions was devot┬Łed to the num┬Łbers 2 through 10 (there was no film for ŌĆ£oneŌĆØ as it is the loneli┬Łest num┬Łber that youŌĆÖll ever do), and was an essen┬Łtial ele┬Łment in Sesame StreetŌĆÖs first sea┬Łson. High┬Łlights include the dream-like ele┬Łva┬Łtor door sequence of ŌĆ£2,ŌĆØ the Jack┬Łson 5 ref┬Łer┬Łence in ŌĆ£5,ŌĆØ and the rac┬Łing fans in ŌĆ£10.ŌĆØ
Slick got involved through her first hus┬Łband, Jer┬Łry Slick, who pro┬Łduced the seg┬Łments for San Fran┬Łcis┬Łco-based ani┬Łma┬Łtion stu┬Łdio Imag┬Łi┬Łna┬Łtion, Inc. Head┬Łed by ani┬Łma┬Łtor Jeff Hale, the com┬Łpa┬Łny also pro┬Łduced the Pin┬Łball seg┬Łments, as well as the famous anamor┬Łphic ŌĆ£Type┬Łwriter Guy,ŌĆØ the Ring┬Łmas┬Łter, and the Detec┬Łtive Man. Hale, by the way, has a cameo as Augie ŌĆ£BenŌĆØ Dog┬Łgie in the well-loved Lucas par┬Ło┬Łdy Hard┬Łware Wars.)
The deliri┬Łous music was com┬Łposed and per┬Łformed by Colum┬Łbia jazz artist Den┬Łny Zeitlin, who would go on to score the 1979 remake of Inva┬Łsion of the Body Snatch┬Łers. Zeitlin plays both piano and clavinet; accom┬Łpa┬Łny┬Łing him is Bob┬Łby Natan┬Łson on drums and Mel Graves on bass. Accord┬Łing to Zeitlin, Grace Slick over┬Łdubbed her vocals lat┬Łer.
This wasnŌĆÖt SlickŌĆÖs first encounter with Jim Hen┬Łson. In 1968, she and oth┬Łer mem┬Łbers of Jef┬Łfer┬Łson Air┬Łplane were part of a coun┬Łter┬Łcul┬Łture doc┬Łu┬Łmen┬Łtary called Youth ŌĆÖ68, the trail┬Łer for which you can groove on here.


	

Sesame Street, with all its pri┬Łma┬Łry col┬Łors, plas┬Łtic mer┬Łchan┬Łdise, and Elmo infes┬Łta┬Łtion, may have lost its edge, but these ear┬Łly works show its rev┬Ło┬Łlu┬Łtion┬Łary foun┬Łda┬Łtions.
Note: An ear┬Łli┬Łer ver┬Łsion of this post appeared on our site in 2015.
Relat┬Łed Con┬Łtent:
Philip Glass Com┬Łpos┬Łes Music for a Sesame Street Ani┬Łma┬Łtion (1979)
Itzhak Perl┬Łman Appears on┬ĀSesame Street┬Āand Poignant┬Łly Shows Kids How to Play the Vio┬Łlin and Push Through LifeŌĆÖs Lim┬Łits (1981)
Watch the First Episode of Sesame Street and 140 Oth┬Łer Free Episodes
A Young Jim Hen┬Łson Teach┬Łes You How to Make Pup┬Łpets with Socks, Ten┬Łnis Balls & Oth┬Łer House┬Łhold Goods (1969)
See Ste┬Łvie Won┬Łder Play ŌĆ£Super┬Łsti┬ŁtionŌĆØ and Ban┬Łter with Grover on Sesame Street in 1973
Thank You, Mask Man: Lenny BruceŌĆÖs Lone Ranger Com┬Łe┬Łdy Rou┬Łtine Becomes a NSFW Ani┬Łmat┬Łed Film (1968)
 Ted Mills is a free┬Łlance writer on the arts.

title: Every Known Work by Georgia OŌĆÖKeeffe Has Been Digitized and Made Available Online
description:
in Archives, Art, Museums |   	March 23rd, 2026  1 Comment
	
			
Upon hear┬Łing the names of Arthur Dove or Mars┬Łden Hart┬Łley, the sat┬Łu┬Łrat┬Łed col┬Ło
body:
in Archives, Art, Museums |   	March 23rd, 2026  1 Comment
	
			
Upon hear┬Łing the names of Arthur Dove or Mars┬Łden Hart┬Łley, the sat┬Łu┬Łrat┬Łed col┬Łors and organ┬Łi┬Łcal┬Łly askew lines of those paintersŌĆÖ land┬Łscapes may appear before your mindŌĆÖs eye. But unless you have a spe┬Łcial inter┬Łest in Amer┬Łi┬Łcan mod┬Łernists of the ear┬Łly twen┬Łti┬Łeth cen┬Łtu┬Łry, they prob┬Ła┬Łbly donŌĆÖt. The name Geor┬Łgia OŌĆÖKe┬Łeffe, by con┬Łtrast, can hard┬Łly fail to bring a few images even to the mind of the strict┬Łly casu┬Łal art appre┬Łci┬Ła┬Łtor: New Mex┬Łi┬Łcan mesas, ani┬Łmal skulls, and above all flow┬Łers in extreme close-up. Apart from the artis┬Łtic skill and dis┬Łtinc┬Łtive vision with which she cre┬Łat┬Łed it, OŌĆÖKe┬Łef┬ŁfeŌĆÖs work per┬Łsists in the wider cul┬Łture because of how well it hap┬Łpens to repro┬Łduce in a vari┬Łety of con┬Łtexts, includ┬Łing post┬Łcards, mugs, and even appar┬Łel, such as that sold at her epony┬Łmous muse┬Łum in San┬Łta Fe.

Keep┬Łing such prod┬Łucts around is, of course, no sub┬Łsti┬Łtute for see┬Łing the real thing; in their phys┬Łi┬Łcal real┬Łi┬Łty, OŌĆÖKe┬Łef┬ŁfeŌĆÖs paint┬Łings have a way of rebuff┬Łing all the inter┬Łpre┬Łta┬Łtions with which theyŌĆÖve been freight┬Łed for more than a cen┬Łtu┬Łry now. If you canŌĆÖt make it out to New Mex┬Łi┬Łco, the Geor┬Łgia OŌĆÖKe┬Łeffe Muse┬Łum has been work┬Łing to make every sin┬Łgle one of her pieces (includ┬Łing sculp┬Łtures and pho┬Łtographs) avail┬Łable for view┬Łing online at a just-launched por┬Łtal called Access OŌĆÖKe┬Łeffe.
The muse┬Łum describes it as a ŌĆ£user-friend┬Łly, search┬Łable web┬Łsite with high-res┬Ło┬Łlu┬Łtion images, visu┬Łal descrip┬Łtions, exhi┬Łbi┬Łtion his┬Łto┬Łries, archival mate┬Łri┬Łals, and research data asso┬Łci┬Łat┬Łed with the artistŌĆÖs two-vol┬Łume cat┬Ła┬Łlogue raison┬Łn├®.ŌĆØ The siteŌĆÖs vis┬Łi┬Łtors ŌĆ£can browse by col┬Łor, shape, or medi┬Łum, explore the con┬Łtext of works cre┬Łat┬Łed before and after a spe┬Łcif┬Łic paint┬Łing, trace his┬Łtoric exhi┬Łbi┬Łtions, cre┬Łate lists of favorites, and down┬Łload images.ŌĆØ

Access OŌĆÖKe┬Łeffe makes it easy to find the artistŌĆÖs most famous paint┬Łings, but also works that may sur┬Łprise view┬Łers who only know her mesas, skulls, and flow┬Łers. Take, for exam┬Łple, such noc┬Łtur┬Łnal┬Łly themed can┬Łvas┬Łes as her ear┬Łly Starlight Night, from 1917, or her late Unti┬Łtled (City Night), from the nine┬Łteen-sev┬Łen┬Łties. OŌĆÖKe┬Łef┬ŁfeŌĆÖs Amer┬Łi┬Łca, we must remem┬Łber, isnŌĆÖt lim┬Łit┬Łed to the desert: though she did spend most of her near┬Łly cen┬Łtu┬Łry-long lifeŌĆÖs sec┬Łond half in New Mex┬Łi┬Łco, it┬Āalso took her from Wis┬Łcon┬Łsin to Vir┬Łginia to Texas to New York, with stints in South Car┬Łoli┬Łna and Hawaii. Giv┬Łen the impor┬Łtance of under┬Łstand┬Łing any artistŌĆÖs con┬Łtexts both geo┬Łgraph┬Łi┬Łcal and social,┬ĀAccess OŌĆÖKe┬Łeffe also pro┬Łvides an archive of arti┬Łfacts and exhi┬Łbi┬Łtions relat┬Łed to the peo┬Łple and orga┬Łni┬Łza┬Łtions asso┬Łci┬Łat┬Łed with her┬ĀŌĆö Arthur Dove and Mars┬Łden Hart┬Łley includ┬Łed.
Relat┬Łed con┬Łtent:
Geor┬Łgia OŌĆÖKeeffe: A Life in Art, a Short Doc┬Łu┬Łmen┬Łtary on the Painter Nar┬Łrat┬Łed by Gene Hack┬Łman
How Geor┬Łgia OŌĆÖKeeffe Became Geor┬Łgia OŌĆÖKeeffe: An Ani┬Łmat┬Łed Video Tells the Sto┬Łry
An Intro┬Łduc┬Łtion to the Paint┬Łing That Changed Geor┬Łgia OŌĆÖKeeffeŌĆÖs Career: RamŌĆÖs Head, White Hol┬Łly┬Łhock-Hills
The Real Geor┬Łgia OŌĆÖKeeffe: The Artist Reveals Her┬Łself in Vin┬Łtage Doc┬Łu┬Łmen┬Łtary Clips
Recipes from the Kitchen of Geor┬Łgia OŌĆÖKeeffe
Alfred Stieglitz: The Elo┬Łquent Eye, a Reveal┬Łing Look at ŌĆ£The Father of Mod┬Łern Pho┬Łtog┬Łra┬ŁphyŌĆØ
Based in Seoul,┬ĀCol┬Łin┬ĀMarshall┬Āwrites and broad┬Łcasts on cities, lan┬Łguage, and cul┬Łture. HeŌĆÖs the author of the newslet┬Łter┬ĀBooks on Cities┬Āas well as the books┬ĀĒĢ£ĻĄŁ ņÜöņĢĮ ĻĖłņ¦Ć┬Ā(No Sum┬Łma┬Łriz┬Łing Korea) and┬ĀKore┬Łan Newtro.┬ĀFol┬Łlow him on the social net┬Łwork for┬Łmer┬Łly known as Twit┬Łter at┬Ā@colinmarshall.

title: Meditate to the Undulations of Baltic Sea Ice in Jan Erik Waider's Hypnotic Videos
description: Jan Erik Waider has a knack for capturing shorelines, volcanic eruptions, and glaciers at their most mesmerizingŌĆöshrouded in mist, glowing in the
body:
Jan Erik Waider has a knack for capturing shorelines, volcanic eruptions, and glaciers at their most mesmerizingŌĆöshrouded in mist, glowing in the darkness, or illuminated by pale northern light. His atmospheric photographs of icy seas and rugged landscapes from Iceland to the Antarctic, focus on dramatic forms and cast remote places into a dreamy ethereality.
Most recently, Waider captured a striking phenomenon in the Baltic Sea, just off the coast of northern Germany. Fresh ice formed a thin layer on the rolling surface, creating faceted, polygon-like shapes that moved gently and rhythmically with the waves without breaking apart.



WaiderŌĆÖs aerial drone perspective creates an otherworldly, almost totally abstract effect. At first glance, it appears as though it could be a minimalist animation highlighting the interactions between water, light, and motion. ŌĆ£Soft evening light, fine crack lines, and shifting tones from warm gold to deep green turned this fleeting moment into a study of structure, depth, and calm,ŌĆØ Waider says.
See more on WaiderŌĆÖs YouTube channel, Instagram, and Behance.

schema.headline: Meditate to the Undulations of Baltic Sea Ice in Jan Erik Waider's Hypnotic Videos

title: How Do Artists Finance Their Lives? Join Us for a Discussion About Mason Currey's New Book
description: As with most conversations about money, understanding how artists fund their practices and lives is rarely discussed and always of intrigue. Mason
body:
As with most conversations about money, understanding how artists fund their practices and lives is rarely discussed and always of intrigue. Mason Currey┬Ādives into this underexplored topic in his new book Making Art and Making a Living: Adventures in Funding a Creative Life. 
Currey is known for documenting the day-to-day routines of hundreds of artists, writers, filmmakers, designers, musicians, and more. Making Art and Making a Living is in the same vein, revealing how family money, day jobs, schemes, and more have buoyed artists through the ages.
ŌĆŗIn partnership with EXPO Chicago and the Chicago Athletic Association, weŌĆÖre thrilled to celebrate the launch of CurreyŌĆÖs new book. Join us on April 8 for a discussion and signing by the author. Tickets include a hardcover copy, and Colossal Members┬Āreceive $10 off with the code in their account.
Find Making Art and Making a Living in the Colossal Shop, and register for the event to save your seat.

March 22, 2026
ArtCraft
Kate Mothes

If it werenŌĆÖt for being so li
body:
ŌĆ£ArtistŌĆÖs Palette Vase.ŌĆØ All images courtesy of the artist, shared with permission
March 22, 2026
ArtCraft
Kate Mothes

If it werenŌĆÖt for being so lightweight and crisp in their facets, Goran KonjevodŌĆÖs elegant vases could at first glance be mistaken for thin porcelain. Crafted instead from precisely folded paper, the works tap into the relationship betweenŌĆöand associations withŌĆömaterial, form, and function. His meticulous origami compositions combine organic forms with nuanced hues and gradients, creating a sense of visual heft and presence from thin, gauzy material.
KonjevodŌĆÖs work was recently included in Art of the Fold at ACCI Gallery, and ŌĆ£Grey Curves VaseŌĆØ and ŌĆ£ArtistŌĆÖs Palette VaseŌĆØ will be part of an exhibition titled The Craft of Paper: Contemporary Takes on Tradition this August at the Robert C. Williams Museum of Papermaking in Atlanta. See more on Instagram.

title: Stephen Morrison's Trompe-L'┼ōil 'Dog World' Paintings Are Fetching
description: Any dog owner can appreciate the kind of unfettered, often visceral reactions canines have to everything from their favorite treats to a scurrying
body:
Any dog owner can appreciate the kind of unfettered, often visceral reactions canines have to everything from their favorite treats to a scurrying squirrel to another dog passing by the window. Their lack of inhibition and legendary fidelity bring comfort, routine, and goofiness to our daily lives despite their total unawareness of their effects on us. For Stephen Morrison, curiosity and play find their way into vibrant, quirky paintings that ŌĆ£invite viewers to rediscover the magic and absurdity often obscured by the routine,ŌĆØ he says.
MorrisonŌĆÖs practice has lately revolved around trompe lŌĆÖ┼ōil compositions of everyday objects and tableaux in which dogsŌĆÖ features appear unexpectedly. A snout stands in for the flap of a handbag or juts out from the side of a Pepsi can. His current solo exhibition, Dog Show #5: Field Recordings at SLAG&RX, centers on a series of objects referencing places he worked on the piecesŌĆöParis, New York City, and MaineŌĆöthat also play important roles in his life.
ŌĆ£111 Limerock StreetŌĆØ (2025), oil on quilted fabric on panel, 79 x 51 inches
MorrisonŌĆÖs own memories and connections find their way into his collection of books, foods, photographs, and other items in an almost seek-and-find fashion. At first glance, the tableaux appear simply as collections of everyday things like vases, fruit, and cameras. But upon closer inspection, tiny visages appear along with references to dogs, from bones stitched into patchwork backgrounds to the sleepy face of a pooch in the center of a starfish and a bunch of green grapes with puppy faces. Always relaxed, even sleepy, the dogsŌĆÖ expressions evoke a calm sweetness, even nostalgia, paired with a sense of abundance.
In this series, the artist grapples with what belonging means, from revisiting his childhood home in Maine to thinking about his past decade in New York City to spending two months in Paris, where, ŌĆ£despite being married to a Frenchman, having many French friends, and having spent considerable time in the city, I had never felt at home,ŌĆØ he says. ŌĆ£The ornate beauty of the architecture and the sense I have of everything being solidly ŌĆśin its placeŌĆÖ makes it hard to feel inspired there for me.ŌĆØ So, he set out to explore that sense of disjointedness and creative conflict.
France is referenced in MorrisonŌĆÖs paintings by backgrounds of toile, or toile de jouy, a fabric design popular in the 18th century that features pastoral scenes, while Maine is represented by patchwork quilts he co-designed with his mother, who actually stitched them before they were incorporated into the works. ŌĆ£By bringing the objects and backgrounds into my dog world, IŌĆÖve rewritten my external material world through this lens, creating a new and more uniquely personal vision of these places,ŌĆØ he says.
Morrison will be an artist-in-residence at BUoY in Tokyo this summer, where heŌĆÖs looking forward to incorporating Japanese textiles into a new series of paintings. HeŌĆÖs also preparing for a pop-up solo exhibition at Lazy Mike Gallery in Seoul and a group exhibition at Hashimoto Contemporary. Dog Show #5: Field Recordings continues through March 28 in New York. See more on the artistŌĆÖs Instagram.
